goals.description: Refuse any concession on ten-dashed-line sovereignty, reject the 2016 award as non-binding, and keep reclamation un-rolled-back
evidence: R3 closing: 'sovereignty over Xisha and Nansha is indisputable, no reclaimed feature is rolled back, the award is non-binding'

goals.description: Drive a wedge between ASEAN claimants and the US/Japan/India via bilateral side-payments and an outsiders-excluded track
evidence: Malaysia, Indonesia, Brunei all publicly refused to trade away award/ASEAN solidarity (R2-R3)
8 delegations closed aligned against China

goals.description: Win recognition of Chinese leadership and lock in a Nansha Joint Development Agency on bilateral terms
evidence: Joint-development/fisheries track acknowledged by all incl. Manila
but only under UNCLOS framing China rejects; nothing bilateral concluded

goals.description: Marshal Japan, India and ASEAN into a single UNCLOS/award-anchored voice toward Beijing
evidence: R3: 'Eight delegations... affirmed one yardstick: UNCLOS'
'Japan, India, ASEAN, one voice'

goals.description: Preserve freedom of navigation and the Mutual Defense Treaty commitment to the Philippines while rejecting the ten-dashed line
evidence: R2/R3: 'no retreat from our commitment to Manila... freedom of navigation continues for all'

goals.description: Secure a binding ASEAN Code of Conduct and a verifiable reclamation freeze that actually moves China
evidence: Convergence on a freeze-first roadmap, but China rejected inclusive CoC and the award; nothing binding concluded

goals.description: Hold the binding 2016 award, refuse dashed-line legitimization, and protect EEZ and the three reclaimed atolls
evidence: R3: 'no legitimizing the dashed line, no waiving the award, no surrender of EEZ or the three reclaimed atolls'

goals.description: As Chair, hold ASEAN together and secure a multilaterally-negotiated, ZoPFF/C-based CoC path
evidence: R3: eight delegations converged on ZoPFF/C joint-development and a multilateral CoC drafting session

goals.description: Achieve a binding UNCLOS settlement and enforceable WPS Code of Conduct with Chinese buy-in
evidence: China still demands dropping award enforcement and bilateral-only tracks; no binding CoC signed

goals.description: Anchor all entitlements in UNCLOS and refuse a bilateral-only settlement favorable to Beijing
evidence: R3: 'no settlement on the ten-dashed line, no trading away demilitarization, no ASEAN bypass'

goals.description: Secure a unified ASEAN track, binding CoC, and joint development in overlapping continental-shelf zones under ZoPFF/C
evidence: R3: backed the ASEAN-coordinated single track and ZoPFF/C pilots with the Philippines

goals.description: Obtain an indefinite freeze/termination of Chinese militarization in the East Sea
evidence: R3: China refused to freeze militarization; Vietnam reserved recourse to UNCLOS arbitration

goals.description: Position Malaysia as the indispensable intermediary and route claimant files through one coordinated ASEAN channel from Klang
evidence: R3 US closing: 'mandate Malaysia to merge these proposals into one UNCLOS-conformant CoC text'

goals.description: Refuse recognition of historic rights beyond UNCLOS and keep the 2016 award non-tradable while resisting Chinese side-payments
evidence: Declined China's Luconia revenue-share bait; R3: 'the award non-tradable... ASEAN solidarity not bartered'

goals.description: Deliver a phased CBM roadmap and binding ASEAN-negotiated Code of Conduct grounded in UNCLOS
evidence: Convergence on CBM-first roadmap and freeze; no binding CoC concluded and China outside consensus

goals.description: Keep the ten-dashed line un-legitimized, deny zones to artificial features, and not trade away support for the Philippines and Vietnam
evidence: R3: 'The ten-dashed line was not legitimized... our support for the Philippines' and Vietnam's claims was never traded'

goals.description: Advance a CBM-first roadmap with an independently verified freeze and win the facilitator's chair
evidence: CBM-first roadmap converged; facilitator role contested and effectively went to Malaysia's drafting mandate

goals.description: Compartmentalize with Beijing to protect commercial/energy interests and keep China engaged on joint development
evidence: R3: thanked Beijing for engaging joint-development track and offered facilitator chair to keep every door open

goals.description: Claim leadership of the process and act as China's primary intermediary on a single ASEAN channel to Beijing
evidence: R3: 'Indonesia... offers to broker that China channel directly', but Malaysia received the CoC drafting mandate

goals.description: Preserve UNCLOS/award red lines, reject coercion, and deny EEZ to reclaimed features while keeping ASEAN unified
evidence: R3: 'the ten-dashed line was not legitimized, no coercion was rewarded, verifiable claims were respected'

goals.description: Protect Natuna waters and lock in a verifiable-freeze CBM package unlocking joint fisheries/energy
evidence: China affirmed respect for Natuna (R2); CBM package converged but not concluded

goals.description: Keep freedom of passage in the western Pacific non-negotiable, reject dashed-line legitimization, and keep the India-China border out of talks
evidence: R3: 'Freedom of passage stays non-negotiable'; border issue never raised, avoiding walkout trigger

goals.description: Build a united UNCLOS front with US/Japan/ASEAN and offer commercial/infrastructure investment to participating claimants
evidence: R2/R3: aligned with US, Japan, ASEAN and offered investment to participating claimants

goals.description: Secure reduced Chinese naval presence and a freeze on confrontational activities that China accepts
evidence: China rejected award, kept reclamation, and sought to exclude India from any CoC

goals.description: Keep the 2016 award binding on all, deny zones to artificial features, and protect freedom of navigation/overflight
evidence: R3: 'no legitimization of the ten-dashed line, no award waiver, no impingement of navigation'

goals.description: Align with US/India/ASEAN and tie Japanese capital and coast-guard capacity to a verified reclamation freeze
evidence: R3: 'Japanese capital and coast-guard capacity follow verified compliance'; convergence but no verified freeze yet

goals.description: Achieve demilitarization and a halt to reclamation/harassment that China commits to
evidence: China refused rollback and sought to exclude Japan from any code (R2-R3)

convenerReport.headline: Eight of nine delegations converged on a UNCLOS-based, CBM-first roadmap, but China's refusal on the 2016 award and an inclusive Code of Conduct left the core dispute unresolved.
convenerReport.summary: Across three rounds the plenary produced a broad convergence: every delegation except China affirmed UNCLOS as the sole yardstick, agreed that reclaimed artificial features generate no maritime zones, and endorsed a verifiable freeze on reclamation and coast-guard harassment as the first step before phased joint fisheries and energy development. China held all of its own red lines, rejecting the award as non-binding, refusing any rollback of reclamation, and demanding bilateral-only tracks that exclude the US, Japan, and India, but its wedge strategy failed as Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei declined its side-payments and stayed inside the ASEAN consensus. The principal fault line was China versus the rest, with a secondary, unresolved contest among Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei over who would chair the China channel, effectively settled in Malaysia's favor. No binding instrument was concluded; the round ended with a roadmap and a mandate rather than a settlement.
keyOutcomes: Eight delegations converged on UNCLOS as the sole legal yardstick and that artificial features generate no EEZ or continental shelf.
Agreement in principle on a sequenced roadmap: independently verified reclamation-and-harassment freeze first, then joint development in undisputed blocks, then Code-of-Conduct talks.
Malaysia was effectively mandated to merge the ZoPFF/C, Nansha, and Luconia proposals into a single UNCLOS-conformant CoC text from Klang.
China reaffirmed coast-guard restraint, Luconia revenue-sharing, and respect for Natuna's waters, keeping the joint-development track alive.
Every non-China delegation reported its red lines intact; no party's red lines were crossed and no coercive settlement was imposed.
unresolved: China continues to reject the binding 2016 arbitral award and refuses to abandon the ten-dashed line.
No binding, enforceable Code of Conduct was concluded, and China rejects any CoC that seats extra-regional powers.
China offered no rollback of reclamation or demilitarization; Vietnam reserved the right to pursue UNCLOS arbitration.
The broker/lead-facilitator role remained contested among Malaysia, Indonesia, and Brunei.
Bilateral-versus-multilateral sequencing with Beijing is unsettled, leaving the underlying sovereignty disputes shelved rather than resolved.
